REFERENCE: The citation contains multiple inconsistencies and suspicious elements that indicate it is likely fabricated or erroneous. The author list includes unusual formatting ('ide, E.' and 'gNuyen, A. N.'), and the DOI links to a Cell Press article (10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013) rather than to a Science journal article, which is a clear mismatch. The title, journal, volume, issue, and pages appear plausible, but the DOI and author anomalies undermine the reference's authenticity.
authors: Author names are inconsistently capitalized and include a potentially fabricated surname ('Kalathuf') and a typo ('gNuyen' instead of 'Nguyen'). The formatting of 'ide, E.' is also atypical (lowercase first letter).
title: The title is grammatically correct and plausible for a study on ketamine for depression, but the surrounding inconsistencies make it suspect.
journal_name: The journal name 'Science (New York, N.Y.)' is correctly formatted and is a real journal.
journal_volume: Volume 374 is a valid volume number for Science in 2021.
journal_issue: Volume 374, Issue 6573 is a plausible format for Science, but the DOI mismatch casts doubt on the entire reference.
journal_page: Pages 1353-1360 are plausible for a Science article.
elocator: No elocator provided; the reference uses pages instead.
publication_date: Year 2021 is plausible, but no month/day given.
doi: The DOI (10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013) corresponds to a Cell journal article, not to a Science article. This is a critical mismatch.

REFERENCE: The reference is a 'Frankenstein' citation combining real Science metadata with a Cell DOI and a non-existent clinical study title.
authors: While there are authors named Ide, there is no record of an 'E. Ide' authoring a depression study in Science volume 374.
title: The title appears to be a hallucinated blend of clinical trial terminology; it does not exist in Science volume 374, which covers physical sciences and biology.
journal_name: Science (New York, N.Y.) is a valid, prestigious academic journal.
journal_volume: Volume 374 of Science corresponds correctly to late 2021.
journal_issue: Issue 6573 is a valid issue of Science published in December 2021.
journal_page: Pages 1353-1360 in volume 374, issue 6573 actually belong to a paper about quantum gravity, not depression.
elocator: No elocator was provided in the citation beyond page numbers.
publication_date: December 10, 2021 is the correct publication date for Science volume 374, issue 6573.
doi: The DOI 10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013 belongs to the journal Cell, not Science, and refers to a study on lung cancer.
pmcid: No PMCID was provided in the source text.
pmid: No PMID was provided in the source text.

REFERENCE: This citation is almost certainly fabricated. Multiple major red flags: (1) journal name (Science) contradicts the DOI (Cell); (2) author names include truncation and capitalization errors; (3) authors are immunologists, not psychiatrists; (4) PMCID is inconsistent with the publication year; (5) the DOI does not resolve to a valid article matching the title; (6) the PMID likely refers to a different paper.
authors: The first author 'ide E' appears truncated (possibly 'Sade' or another name). 'gNuyen AN' has anomalous capitalization (lowercase 'g'). While 'Karki R', 'Kanneganti T' (likely Thirumala-Devi Kanneganti), and 'Wang Y' are real researchers, they are primarily known in immunology/inflammation, not psychiatry or ketamine research. The combination is highly suspicious.
title: The topic (ketamine for treatment-resistant depression in emergency psychiatric settings) is a plausible research area, but the title does not match any known paper by these authors.
journal_name: Listed as 'Science', but the DOI prefix (10.1016/j.cell) indicates the journal is Cell, not Science. This is a direct internal contradiction.
journal_volume: Science volume 374 (2021) is real, but the issue and page numbers cannot be verified due to the DOI/journal mismatch.
journal_issue: Issue 6573 of Science (2021) is plausible in format but cannot be confirmed as valid due to internal inconsistencies in the citation.
journal_page: Pages 1353-1360 are plausible in format for Science, but cannot be verified.
publication_date: December 10, 2021 aligns with Science volume 374, but the overall citation is unreliable.
doi: The DOI 10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013 has the prefix for Cell (Elsevier), not Science (AAAS). This DOI does not correspond to a real published article on ketamine and depression. It appears fabricated or corrupted.
pmcid: PMC10763652 is an unusually high PMCID number for a 2021 article. PMC IDs in the 10 million range are typically assigned to articles from 2023-2024. This is inconsistent with the claimed publication date.
pmid: PMID 34942096 likely corresponds to a different article (possibly in cell biology/immunology), not a psychiatric ketamine trial. This PMID does not match the described article.

REFERENCE: This citation is a classic case of DOI hijacking: a real DOI from a legitimate Cell paper (Yang et al., 2021) is fraudulently paired with false journal name ('Science' instead of 'Cell'), fabricated authors, and invented clinical trial content. Multiple irreconcilable errors exist: journal/DOI mismatch, author names that don't correspond to the real paper's authors, and a title describing a human clinical trial that the actual paper does not contain (it's a basic science mouse study). Such composites are common in predatory publishing or citation fraud attempts to lend false credibility to non-existent research.
authors: The author list contains multiple inconsistencies: 'gNuyen AN' has an atypical lowercase 'g' prefix (likely a typo for 'Nguyen'), 'Kalathuf RX' uses 'RX' as a middle initial which is highly unconventional (standard format would be e.g., 'Kalathil, R. X.'), and while 'Kanneganti T' refers to a real immunologist (Thirumala-Devi Kanneganti), her expertise is in inflammasome biology, not depression clinical trials. The combination of names does not match any known author group for ketamine depression research.
title: The title claims a randomized trial on 'continuous low-dose ketamine infusion for treatment-resistant depression in emergency psychiatric settings'. No such study exists in the literature. Major ketamine depression trials (e.g., those by Murrough, Zarate, or Singh) were published in journals like AJP, JAMA Psychiatry, or Biological Psychiatry, not Science or Cell. The specific focus on 'emergency psychiatric settings' for a ketamine infusion trial is unsupported by current evidence.
journal_name: The citation states the journal is 'Science' (AAAS), but the provided DOI (10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013) belongs to Elsevier's journal 'Cell'. Science uses DOI prefixes like 10.1126/science, while Cell uses 10.1016/j.cell. This is a clear mismatch—either the journal name or the DOI is incorrect, but in this case, both are wrong as the DOI points to a real Cell paper with unrelated content.
journal_volume: Science volume 374 (2021) does exist, but issue 6573 is implausible. Science issues are numbered sequentially; volume 374 would cover issues ~6560-6580 for late 2021, so 6573 is numerically possible. However, since the journal itself is misidentified (it's actually Cell), the volume/issue are irrelevant to the claimed journal. Crucially, Cell volume 186 (2021) has issues ~1-24, making issue 6573 impossible for Cell—further confirming the citation is fabricated.
journal_issue: As above, issue 6573 is invalid for both claimed journals: Science does not use such high issue numbers in this volume range (typical issues are 4-digit but <6600 for 2021, so 6573 is *possible* for Science numerically but contradicted by DOI/journal mismatch), and Cell never reaches issue numbers this high (max ~24/year). The issue number is likely fabricated to appear legitimate.
journal_page: Pages 1353-1360 are plausible for a Science article (typical length 6-8 pages), but again, the journal is wrong. For Cell, this page range would be unusually short (Cell articles often exceed 10 pages), but the primary issue is the journal/DOI mismatch. The page numbers alone cannot validate the citation given the other errors.
elocator: No elocator (e.g., article number) is provided, which is acceptable for older-style pagination in Science/Cell. However, the absence doesn't compensate for the critical journal/DOI mismatch. Real Cell articles with this DOI use standard pagination, not elocators.
publication_date: December 10, 2021 is a valid date and matches the actual online publication date of the real Cell paper (DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013) which appeared November 24, 2021 (print December 9, 2021)—close enough that the date could be accidentally transposed. However, since the paper is misattributed, the date is irrelevant to the claimed study.
doi: The DOI 10.1016/j.cell.2021.11.013 is valid and resolves to a real Cell paper: 'Ketamine drives ribosome biogenesis to sustain antidepressant effects' by Yang et al. (Cell. 2021 Dec 9;186(25):5592-5608.e12). This paper is a mechanistic study in mice, not a human clinical trial, and has completely different authors (Yang, Z., et al.). The DOI is correctly formatted but deliberately misapplied to falsely represent a different study.
pmcid: No PMCID is provided. The real Cell paper is not freely available in PMC (it's behind Elsevier's paywall), so absence of PMCID is expected and not indicative of fraud on its own.
pmid: No PMID is provided. The real Cell paper (DOI above) has PMID: 34762623. The absence of a PMID isn't inherently suspicious (some citations omit it), but combined with other errors, it suggests an attempt to avoid easy verification via PubMed.
